item.title: Donald Trump’s dangerous ‘war of whim’
item.description: Anyone who claims to know where this conflict will go is bluffing

item.title: What’s a safe asset now?
item.description: Most of the old standbys are not working

item.title: Britain’s ‘open’ voters need a champion
item.description: Despite their triumph in Gorton, it’s not clear the Greens have what it takes to prevent a Reform victory at the next election

item.title: B&M tries to win back Britain’s discount shoppers
item.description: Retail chain’s new chief executive needs to catch up with rival Home Bargains

item.title: France’s groundbreaking nuclear offer to Europe
item.description: Macron’s decision to extend the deterrent is a boost for the continent’s defence

item.title: AES’s private equity buyers pull off the self-leveraging buyout
item.description: Investors in power and utility group might have expected more but it’s not clear they had a right to

item.title: Ghana’s ageing cocoa farmers are caught in a price trap
item.description: Small farms and low productivity mean their livelihoods did not improve even as markets hit a record high

item.title: Airlines’ healthy finances will be tested by Middle East conflict
item.description: Carriers have enjoyed strong demand and cheap fuel but escalating tensions threaten both

item.title: Iran war is upending gas markets
item.description: The disruption to LNG flows has sparked concerns about supply to the key regional customers of Europe and Asia

item.title: Hormuz crisis is a wake-up call for complacent gas consumers
item.description: The world has already reckoned with its dependence on oil but retains other energy vulnerabilities

item.title: Can the US build ships again?
item.description: Not if Trump refuses to follow his own administration’s best advice

item.title: How western navies can keep the Strait of Hormuz open
item.description: Although the narrow waterway is easy for Iran to block, playing offence could prevent global economic repercussions

item.title: Iran attacks show US haven status is in peril
item.description: In moments of extreme uncertainty investors usually rush to the safety of US government bonds, but not this time

item.title: On lead, Britain is the laggard of the developed world
item.description: The UK’s exposure surveillance system for this public health menace is reactive — unlike elsewhere

item.title: The war and markets
item.description: Time is the crucial factor

item.title: The great British electricity puzzle
item.description: It turns out that making the transition to clean energy while keeping the price down is hard

item.title: The ‘stick or twist’ moment for UK banks
item.description: Will high street lenders persist with disciplined growth plans or take riskier bets to boost fee income

item.title: Blacklisting Russian paramilitaries will not work on its own
item.description: A foreign terrorist designation without secondary sanctions and sustained pressure is unlikely to succeed

item.title: Trump’s epic gamble in the Middle East
item.description: The US president’s goal of regime change augurs immense regional chaos

item.title: Iran war will test the vulnerability of oil markets
item.description: While there are buffers of supply, spare capacity will come under pressure in the event of any further escalation

item.title: Trump has no realistic plan for Iran’s future
item.description: The US president has not understood the lessons of past wars for regime change in Iraq and Afghanistan

item.title: Britain’s rising tax on ambition
item.description: The country’s tax system and rising costs sap the returns to hard work

item.title: The cost of UK higher education doesn’t add up
item.description: Complexity has been introduced by politicians and as a result there are five different repayment plans

item.title: Why it makes sense for women to work for women
item.description: The numbers show pay gaps are lower when the CEO is a woman. Shame there are so few of them

item.title: What a chocolate company can tell us about OpenAI’s risk
item.description: For the closest American analogue to its unusual charitable structure, you need to look at Hershey
